name: neg_6_disjointness
property: allocated_addrs and free_addrs are not necessarily disjoint
reason: An address in both sets means it is treated as both allocated and free — a use-after-free vulnerability
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_6_disjointness_fresh_slab
property: allocated_addrs and free_addrs are not necessarily disjoint
reason: If disjointness is not preserved even from a trivially-disjoint initial state, the invariant is fundamentally broken
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_6_disjointness_after_cycle
property: allocated_addrs and free_addrs are not necessarily disjoint
reason: Alloc-dealloc cycling could introduce an address into both sets if the invariant does not enforce disjointness
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_6_disjointness_interleaved
property: allocated_addrs and free_addrs are not necessarily disjoint
reason: Multiple interleaved alloc/dealloc operations are the most likely scenario to expose set overlap if inv() is weak
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_7_unaligned_dealloc
property: deallocate succeeds for a pointer that is not aligned to block_size
reason: An unaligned pointer entering free_addrs corrupts the allocator's alignment invariant for future allocations
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_7_unaligned_dealloc_normal
property: deallocate succeeds for a pointer that is not aligned to block_size
reason: If allocated_addrs only contains aligned addresses AND ptr is in allocated_addrs, unalignment should be contradictory
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_7_unaligned_dealloc_weak_inv
property: deallocate succeeds for a pointer that is not aligned to block_size
reason: If inv() does not enforce alignment of all addresses in allocated_addrs, unaligned pointers can leak into free_addrs
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_8_double_free_err_code
property: double-free is not prevented if the same pointer is somehow in allocated_addrs twice (set semantics should prevent this, but the error path allows silent no-ops)
reason: An unconstrained error code on the Err path means callers cannot programmatically detect or handle double-free attempts
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_8_double_free_err_code_just_freed
property: double-free is not prevented if the same pointer is somehow in allocated_addrs twice (set semantics should prevent this, but the error path allows silent no-ops)
reason: After deallocation, re-freeing the same pointer should produce a specific diagnosable error, not an arbitrary one
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_8_double_free_err_code_never_allocated
property: double-free is not prevented if the same pointer is somehow in allocated_addrs twice (set semantics should prevent this, but the error path allows silent no-ops)
reason: Freeing a never-allocated pointer is a severe bug that should produce a distinguishable error code
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_8_double_free_err_code_null
property: double-free is not prevented if the same pointer is somehow in allocated_addrs twice (set semantics should prevent this, but the error path allows silent no-ops)
reason: Freeing a null pointer should produce a specific, documented error code to aid debugging
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_9_free_count_unconstrained
property: from_raw_parts does not constrain the total number of free blocks relative to the memory region size
reason: A slab reporting 1 free block in a region large enough for 10+ wastes most of the memory region
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_9_free_count_single_block
property: from_raw_parts does not constrain the total number of free blocks relative to the memory region size
reason: When len equals block_size, at most one block fits, but the spec does not bound free_addrs cardinality
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_9_free_count_large_region
property: from_raw_parts does not constrain the total number of free blocks relative to the memory region size
reason: Claiming more free blocks than physically fit in the memory region is a logical impossibility the spec should exclude
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_9_free_count_non_multiple
property: from_raw_parts does not constrain the total number of free blocks relative to the memory region size
reason: Counting a partial trailing block as a full free block would allow allocation of memory beyond the region boundary
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_10_always_same_addr
property: allocate could always pick the same address from free_addrs even when multiple are available
reason: Always returning the same address starves all other free blocks and wastes allocator capacity
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_10_always_same_addr_single
property: allocate could always pick the same address from free_addrs even when multiple are available
reason: With a single free block there is no choice — this scenario should trivially verify as a baseline
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_10_always_same_addr_multiple
property: allocate could always pick the same address from free_addrs even when multiple are available
reason: When multiple blocks are available, always returning start_addr means subsequent blocks are never used
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_10_always_same_addr_recycled
property: allocate could always pick the same address from free_addrs even when multiple are available
reason: Repeated alloc-dealloc on the same block with other blocks available indicates degenerate no-progress behavior
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_1_always_err
property: from_raw_parts always returns Err(InvalidArgument) regardless of input validity
reason: A trivially failing implementation satisfies the spec vacuously on the Ok branch; valid inputs should succeed
params: result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_1_always_err_valid_inputs
property: from_raw_parts always returns Err(InvalidArgument) regardless of input validity — when len > 0, block_size > 0, len >= block_size
reason: With seemingly valid size parameters the spec still permits unconditional Err, blocking all slab creation
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_1_always_err_aligned_addr
property: from_raw_parts always returns Err(InvalidArgument) regardless of input validity — when addr is non-null and properly aligned
reason: Even with a non-null, properly aligned address and valid sizes, the spec still permits unconditional failure
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_2_zero_block_size
property: from_raw_parts succeeds with block_size == 0
reason: A slab with block_size 0 makes alignment checks (addr % block_size) undefined; the spec should reject this
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_2_zero_block_size_zero_len
property: from_raw_parts succeeds with block_size == 0 and len == 0
reason: A degenerate slab with zero block_size and zero length is meaningless; spec should require block_size > 0
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_3_empty_free_addrs
property: from_raw_parts succeeds but produces a slab with free_addrs == empty set
reason: A slab with capacity (len >= block_size) but no free addresses is useless; every allocate call would fail
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_3_empty_free_addrs_small_len
property: from_raw_parts succeeds but produces a slab with free_addrs == empty when len < block_size
reason: Returning Ok for a region too small to hold one block is misleading; should return Err instead
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_3_empty_free_addrs_zero_region
property: from_raw_parts succeeds but produces a slab where start_addr == end_addr (zero-sized region)
reason: A zero-sized region guarantees no usable blocks; spec should not report Ok for an empty slab
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_4_zero_capacity_slab
property: from_raw_parts succeeds with start_addr == end_addr (zero-capacity slab)
reason: len == 0 trivially satisfies start_addr >= addr and end_addr <= addr + 0; a zero-capacity slab is useless
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_4_zero_capacity_alignment_waste
property: from_raw_parts succeeds with start_addr == end_addr due to alignment waste when len > 0
reason: Non-zero len but start == end means all space was wasted; spec allows Ok for a slab with no usable region
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_4_zero_capacity_block_too_large
property: from_raw_parts succeeds with start_addr == end_addr when block_size > len
reason: block_size exceeds available memory; no blocks can be carved out yet spec permits Ok with empty region
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_5_addr_outside_range
property: allocate returns a pointer that is outside the slab's [start_addr, end_addr) range
reason: Returning an address outside [start_addr, end_addr) means writing to memory the slab does not own — a soundness hole
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_5_addr_outside_range_fresh_slab
property: allocate returns a pointer outside the slab's range on a freshly created slab
reason: On a fresh slab (no prior allocations), free_addrs is unconstrained by from_raw_parts, so out-of-range addresses may be returned
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: gap_1_free_addrs_complete
property: On success, free_addrs equals the complete set of all block-aligned addresses in [start_addr, end_addr). The spec only says allocated_addrs == Set::empty() — it says nothing about free_addrs, so a caller cannot know any blocks are available to allocate.
reason: A freshly-created slab with empty allocated_addrs but also empty free_addrs has zero usable capacity, making subsequent allocate() always fail
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: gap_1_free_addrs_complete_min_slab
property: On success, free_addrs equals the complete set of all block-aligned addresses in [start_addr, end_addr). The spec only says allocated_addrs == Set::empty() — it says nothing about free_addrs, so a caller cannot know any blocks are available to allocate.
reason: With len just above 2*block_size, the body guarantees at least 1 data block is free, but the spec cannot prove it
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: gap_1_free_addrs_complete_large
property: On success, free_addrs equals the complete set of all block-aligned addresses in [start_addr, end_addr). The spec only says allocated_addrs == Set::empty() — it says nothing about free_addrs, so a caller cannot know any blocks are available to allocate.
reason: A slab over a region large enough for ~100 blocks reporting only 1 free block wastes nearly all provided memory
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: gap_2_loose_addr_bounds
property: The spec gives only a loose bound on start_addr (>= addr) and end_addr (<= addr + len). The body computes exact values. A caller cannot determine the usable address range or number of allocatable blocks.
reason: The loose bounds allow start_addr and end_addr to collapse the usable region to almost nothing despite ample input memory
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: gap_2_loose_addr_bounds_non_multiple
property: The spec gives only a loose bound on start_addr (>= addr) and end_addr (<= addr + len). The body computes exact values. A caller cannot determine the usable address range or number of allocatable blocks.
reason: When len is not a multiple of block_size, end_addr should be addr+(len/block_size)*block_size, but the spec allows end_addr to collapse to addr
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: gap_2_loose_addr_bounds_large_block
property: The spec gives only a loose bound on start_addr (>= addr) and end_addr (<= addr + len). The body computes exact values. A caller cannot determine the usable address range or number of allocatable blocks.
reason: When block_size is large relative to len, the exact index overhead matters, but the spec allows all space consumed by index
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: gap_3_valid_inputs_no_success_guarantee
property: The spec's Err case only guarantees e.code == ErrorCode::InvalidArgument. It does not specify which input conditions cause failure, so a caller providing valid inputs cannot prove the call succeeds.
reason: A caller with valid inputs cannot prove Ok is returned, making it impossible to prove downstream allocations are reachable
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: gap_3_valid_inputs_no_success_guarantee_null_addr
property: The spec's Err case only guarantees e.code == ErrorCode::InvalidArgument. It does not specify which input conditions cause failure, so a caller providing valid inputs cannot prove the call succeeds.
reason: The spec allows Ok even for null addr because it has no requires clause linking input validity to the Ok branch
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: gap_3_valid_inputs_no_success_guarantee_blocksize_gt_len
property: The spec's Err case only guarantees e.code == ErrorCode::InvalidArgument. It does not specify which input conditions cause failure, so a caller providing valid inputs cannot prove the call succeeds.
reason: The spec allows Ok even when block_size > len because it has no requires/ensures linking this condition to the Err branch
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: gap_4_lowest_addr_not_guaranteed
property: The body always returns the lowest-indexed free block (first-fit). The spec only says the returned address is some member of free_addrs — it does not specify which element is chosen.
reason: The body guarantees first-fit (lowest address), but the spec allows any free address, so callers cannot rely on deterministic allocation order
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: gap_4_lowest_addr_not_guaranteed_singleton
property: The body always returns the lowest-indexed free block (first-fit). The spec only says the returned address is some member of free_addrs — it does not specify which element is chosen.
reason: With a singleton free set, first-fit is trivially satisfied; this scenario should be rejected (no smaller element exists)
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: gap_4_lowest_addr_not_guaranteed_fresh_slab
property: The body always returns the lowest-indexed free block (first-fit). The spec only says the returned address is some member of free_addrs — it does not specify which element is chosen.
reason: First allocation on a fresh slab should return start_addr (lowest block), but the spec allows any free address
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: gap_5_error_code_unconstrained
property: On error, the spec says Err(_) without constraining the error code or message. Callers cannot pattern-match on the error to distinguish 'slab full' from other failures.
reason: A caller handling 'slab full' distinctly from other errors cannot rely on any specific error code from the spec
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: gap_5_error_code_unconstrained_full_slab
property: On error, the spec says Err(_) without constraining the error code or message. Callers cannot pattern-match on the error to distinguish 'slab full' from other failures.
reason: When the slab is full, the body returns a specific error from Bitmap::alloc, but the spec allows any error code including unrelated ones
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: gap_6_err_code_unconstrained
property: On error, the body returns ErrorCode::BadAddress for all three failure modes (out-of-bounds, unaligned, already-free). The spec only says Err(_) and !allocated_addrs.contains(ptr), hiding the specific error code entirely.
reason: Callers cannot rely on the error code being BadAddress since the spec uses Err(_) wildcard, hiding all three failure modes behind an unconstrained error
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: gap_6_err_code_unconstrained_oob
property: On error, the body returns ErrorCode::BadAddress for all three failure modes (out-of-bounds, unaligned, already-free). The spec only says Err(_) and !allocated_addrs.contains(ptr), hiding the specific error code entirely.
reason: When ptr is out of bounds, the body returns BadAddress but the spec allows any error code, so callers cannot distinguish out-of-bounds from other failures
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: gap_6_err_code_unconstrained_unaligned
property: On error, the body returns ErrorCode::BadAddress for all three failure modes (out-of-bounds, unaligned, already-free). The spec only says Err(_) and !allocated_addrs.contains(ptr), hiding the specific error code entirely.
reason: When ptr is unaligned, the body returns BadAddress but the spec allows any error code, preventing callers from diagnosing alignment issues
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: gap_6_err_code_unconstrained_double_free
property: On error, the body returns ErrorCode::BadAddress for all three failure modes (out-of-bounds, unaligned, already-free). The spec only says Err(_) and !allocated_addrs.contains(ptr), hiding the specific error code entirely.
reason: When ptr is a double-free, the body returns BadAddress but the spec allows any error code, preventing callers from detecting double-free bugs
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: gap_7_totality_missing
property: The union of allocated_addrs and free_addrs always equals the complete set of block-aligned addresses in [start_addr, end_addr). This totality invariant is maintained by the View implementation but SlabView::inv() only requires disjointness, not coverage.
reason: Without totality, free_addrs == empty does not imply all blocks are allocated, so a caller cannot conclude the slab is truly full when allocate returns Err
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: gap_7_totality_missing_empty_alloc
property: The union of allocated_addrs and free_addrs always equals the complete set of block-aligned addresses in [start_addr, end_addr). This totality invariant is maintained by the View implementation but SlabView::inv() only requires disjointness, not coverage.
reason: With both sets empty in a non-trivial slab, all block addresses are untracked — the spec should guarantee free_addrs covers all blocks when allocated_addrs is empty
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: gap_7_totality_missing_after_ops
property: The union of allocated_addrs and free_addrs always equals the complete set of block-aligned addresses in [start_addr, end_addr). This totality invariant is maintained by the View implementation but SlabView::inv() only requires disjointness, not coverage.
reason: After alloc/dealloc cycles, free_addrs empty plus untracked block addresses means the slab lost capacity — callers expect allocate Err to mean all blocks are in allocated_addrs
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: gap_8_invalid_ptr_accepted
property: The body validates three distinct preconditions sequentially (bounds, alignment, already-free) before modifying state. Deallocate is safe to call with ANY pointer. The spec's Err branch preserves state, but doesn't guarantee that out-of-range or unaligned pointers are always rejected via Err.
reason: If the spec allows Ok for an invalid pointer, deallocate could corrupt state by moving a bogus address into free_addrs
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: gap_8_invalid_ptr_accepted_oob
property: The body validates three distinct preconditions sequentially (bounds, alignment, already-free) before modifying state. Deallocate is safe to call with ANY pointer. The spec's Err branch preserves state, but doesn't guarantee that out-of-range or unaligned pointers are always rejected via Err.
reason: If allocated_addrs can contain out-of-range addresses, deallocate would "succeed" for wild pointers, corrupting free_addrs with invalid addresses
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: gap_8_invalid_ptr_accepted_unaligned
property: The body validates three distinct preconditions sequentially (bounds, alignment, already-free) before modifying state. Deallocate is safe to call with ANY pointer. The spec's Err branch preserves state, but doesn't guarantee that out-of-range or unaligned pointers are always rejected via Err.
reason: If allocated_addrs can contain unaligned addresses, deallocate would insert an unaligned address into free_addrs, breaking the allocator's alignment guarantees
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: gap_8_invalid_ptr_accepted_double_free
property: The body validates three distinct preconditions sequentially (bounds, alignment, already-free) before modifying state. Deallocate is safe to call with ANY pointer. The spec's Err branch preserves state, but doesn't guarantee that out-of-range or unaligned pointers are always rejected via Err.
reason: If allocated_addrs and free_addrs can overlap, a double-free returns Ok instead of Err, silently corrupting the allocator's bookkeeping
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>
